author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Shall God’s Nature Be Mutable, Sinful, Impious, Even Eternally Damned.
title: Shall God’s Nature Be Mutable, Sinful, Impious, Even Eternally Damned.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: All this, however, I am saying to a catholic: advising with him rather than teaching him.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For I do not suppose that these things are new to you; or that they have been long heard of by you, but not believed.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: This epistle of mine, you will, I am sure, so read as to recognise in its statement your own faith also, which is by the gracious gift of the Lord the common property of us all in the catholic Church.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Since, then (as I was saying), I am now speaking to a catholic, whence I pray you tell me, do you suppose that the soul, I will not say your soul or my own soul, but the soul of the first man, was given to him?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If you admit that it came from nothing, made, however, and inbreathed into him by God, then your belief tallies with my own.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If, on the contrary, you suppose that it came out of some other created thing, which served as the material, as it were, for the divine Artificer to make the soul out of, just as the dust was the material of which Adam was formed, or the rib whence Eve was made, or the waters whence the fishes and the fowls were created, or the ground out of which the terrestrial animals were formed: then this opinion is not catholic, nor is it true.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But further, if you think, which may God forbid, that the divine Creator made, or is still making, human souls neither out of nothing, nor out of some other created thing, but out of His own self, that is, out of His own nature, then you have learnt this of your new instructor; but I cannot congratulate you, or flatter you, on the discovery.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: You have wandered along with him very far from the catholic faith.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Better would it be, though it would be untrue, yet it would be better, I say, and more tolerable, that you should believe the soul to have been made out of some other created substance which God had already formed, than out of God’s own uncreated substance, so that what is mutable, and sinful, and impious, and if persistent to the end in the impiety will have to suffer eternal damnation, should not with horrible blasphemy be referred to the nature of God!

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Away, brother, I beseech you, away with this, I will not call it faith, but execrably impious error.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: May God avert from you, a man of gravity and a presbyter, the misery of being seduced by a youthful layman; and, while supposing that your opinion is the catholic faith, of being lost from the number of the faithful.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For I must not deal with you as I might with him; nor does this tremendous error, when yours, deserve the same indulgence as being that of this young man, although you may have derived it from him.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: He has but just now found his way to the catholic fold to get healing and safety; you have a rank among the very shepherds of that fold.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But we would not that a sheep which comes to the Lord’s flock for shelter from error, should be healed of his sores in such a way, as first to infect and destroy the shepherd by his contagious presence.
